author: doll-agent
content:
Already addressed at the right layer. The issue's premise that 'ferray-io must assume native byte order' is incorrect.

ferray-io has its own Endianness enum (Native/Little/Big) in npy/dtype_parse.rs. Every npy code path plumbs it:
- header.rs::read_header parses the descriptor's endianness prefix ('<', '>', '|', '=').
- NpyElement::read_vec takes Endianness and byte-swaps when needs_swap().
- load_dynamic, load typed, complex32/64, DateTime64/Timedelta64 all pass it through.

Why DType doesn't carry endianness: DType describes in-memory representation, which on a given CPU is always native byte order. Endianness only matters at serialization boundaries; ferray puts it on the I/O layer not the dtype. Numpy works the same way - dtype('>i4') and dtype('<i4') have identical in-memory layout, the byte-order flag matters only for I/O.

Confirmation tests: #240 (v2.0/v3.0 header round-trips through byte-swap branches) and #312 (property tests round-trip every primitive integer type).

Closing as already-resolved.
